questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult technical decision under pressure. What was the situation and outcome?
key_points: Specific situation with real stakes
Clear decision criteria you used
Action you took and why
Measurable outcome or result
What you learned from it

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to push back on a request from a senior leader. How did you handle it?
key_points: Context: what was being asked and why you disagreed
How you communicated your concerns respectfully
Data or evidence you used to support your position
Resolution: compromise, escalation, or outcome
Relationship impact: maintained trust despite disagreement

questions.question: Tell me about a time you built a team or capability from scratch. What was your approach?
key_points: What needed to be built and why
Hiring or talent strategy
How you structured roles and responsibilities
Mentoring and skill development approach
Measurable outcomes (team size, delivery metrics, etc.)

questions.question: Describe a project that failed or didn't meet expectations. What happened and what did you learn?
key_points: Honest acknowledgment of what went wrong
Your role and responsibility in the failure
Root cause analysis (not blame-shifting)
Specific changes you made afterward
How it made you a better leader/engineer

questions.question: How do you balance security requirements with innovation speed in your teams?
key_points: Make the secure path the easy path (shift left)
Automate compliance so it doesn't slow delivery
Risk-based approach: not everything needs the same rigor
Concrete examples of enabling velocity while maintaining security
Cultural approach: security as enabler, not blocker

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to learn a new technology or domain quickly. How did you approach it?
key_points: What you needed to learn and the timeline
Your learning strategy (structured study, hands-on, mentoring)
How you validated your understanding
How you applied the new knowledge in practice
Outcome and ongoing learning approach

questions.question: Describe how you handle a situation where team members disagree on a technical approach.
key_points: Listen to all perspectives before deciding
Use objective criteria (data, benchmarks, constraints)
Make a clear decision and communicate reasoning
Ensure buy-in from the team after the decision
Follow up to validate the decision was correct

questions.question: How do you ensure your team stays current with rapidly evolving technology?
key_points: Dedicated learning time or budget
Knowledge sharing sessions within the team
Conference attendance or community participation
Hands-on experimentation and PoCs
Mentoring and cross-training

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to deliver results with limited resources or a tight deadline.
key_points: Specific constraints you faced
How you prioritized (what you did and didn't do)
How you communicated trade-offs to stakeholders
Creative solutions or efficiency improvements
Outcome and what you delivered

questions.question: How would you build a security culture in an organization that historically prioritizes speed over security?
key_points: Start with quick wins that show security doesn't slow things down
Automate security checks into existing workflows
Educate through real examples, not fear
Make security metrics visible to leadership
Champion security advocates within development teams
Celebrate security improvements alongside feature delivery

questions.question: Why platform engineering, for you?
key_points: A specific moment or experience that taught you platform work matters to you — not generic 'I like infrastructure'
The shape of the work you enjoy most: designing, implementing, enabling, teaching — name your center of gravity
The loop you want to optimize for — typically 'I build something, another engineer I don't know uses it months later, they tell me it made their work better'
Honest trade-off: what you're giving up by moving into this role (operational clarity of SRE, the adrenaline of incident response) and why that trade-off is right for you now
Anti-pattern to avoid: generic career-mechanical answer ('next step for growth') or framework-heavy answer (citing CNCF reports). The answer must be specifically yours
Length: 1-2 minutes. Feels natural, not rehearsed

questions.question: Why this move, why now?
key_points: A specific catalyst or moment — a reorg, a project that changed your sense of what you want, a mentor's comment, a role you watched unfold
Evidence of preparation — something concrete: reading you've done, a side project, conversations with platform engineers at 4+ companies, the 12-week self-directed curriculum you completed
Why not 2 years ago — what you were learning then that was valuable; honest, not self-deprecating
Why not 2 years from now — the overlap between SRE experience and platform role is sharp right now; in 2 years you'd be further from operational work and translation would be harder
Anti-pattern: 'the market is strong' (external-driven, reveals no internal push); 'I've been thinking about it for a while' (vague)
Length: 1-2 minutes

questions.question: Tell me about something you built that didn't get the adoption you hoped for. What happened?
key_points: What you built and what adoption you expected (concrete numbers)
What you assumed about users that turned out to be wrong — and how you learned it was wrong
The signal that told you it wasn't landing — and how long it took you to see it (honest signal-latency)
What you did about it: kept iterating, rescoped, deprecated, learned and moved on
The second-order takeaway: what your process for building platform features has changed since
Anti-pattern: 'teams were resistant to change' (blame-shifting, disqualifying); 'it was used, just not as much as hoped' (hedging)
Strongest version: realization that you built the right tool for the wrong target state — a sign of systemic reflection

questions.question: Tell me about a time you built an abstraction that turned out to be the wrong abstraction. How did you recognize it, and what did you do?
key_points: The problem you were solving and why the abstraction seemed right at the time (fairly described — wrong abstractions usually look right initially)
The early success — don't pretend it was obviously wrong from the start
The pattern of pressure — requests that felt like fighting the abstraction rather than using it
The recognition moment — specific and concrete, not 'over time we realized'
The decision: evolve, replace, or deprecate
What you took away about how to recognize this pattern earlier next time — a pattern detection heuristic you now apply
Example pattern to learn: 'when teams ask for custom or hybrid variants of a tier, the abstraction is collapsing'

questions.question: Describe a situation where a platform problem was brewing for months and you didn't see it until it became a real issue. What was the signal, and why did you miss it?
key_points: The problem that emerged and its consequence
The signal that had been visible but missed — ideally qualitative or systemic, not a standard metric
Honest reflection on why you missed it — attention blind spot, over-focus on aggregate metrics instead of per-tenant, bias toward happy-path signals
The consequence: what the missed signal cost in engineering hours, trust erosion, or business impact
What you changed about your attention habit afterward — a specific practice
No self-flagellation — appropriate weight, not performative regret
Strong version: the signal was in per-tenant trends you weren't looking at (only aggregate), or in a qualitative complaint pattern you didn't pattern-match to

questions.question: Walk me through a platform feature you shipped and then drove to adoption. What did the rollout actually look like, week by week?
key_points: Pre-launch design partners — specific teams that had input and shaped the design before GA
Launch criteria — what made you call it 'ready for general adoption,' beyond 'it compiles'
First 30 days: who adopted early, what friction emerged, what you fixed fast
Plateau-breaking move: the specific tactic that moved you past early-adopter-wall — often matching the new thing's UX to the existing copy-paste workflow
Long tail handling: the holdouts — did you make peace or keep pushing?
Adoption metric beyond count: depth of use, retention, whether teams came back
Anti-pattern: 'we announced it in all-hands' (no real product thinking about rollout)

questions.question: Tell me about a time you convinced someone who was initially skeptical of a platform direction. What was their objection, and what actually changed their mind?
key_points: Steelman the skeptic's objection — describe it fairly, not as a strawman
Acknowledge why their objection was legitimate — they were right about a real risk
The specific moment or evidence that changed their mind — usually not data; usually a demo, a pilot, a witnessed failure mode addressed, or a redesigned approach reflecting their concern
Outcome: did you earn a supporter, a neutral, or a tolerator? Be specific
What you took away from the skeptic's objection — did it reshape what you built?
Anti-pattern: 'I showed them the data' (reveals shallow understanding of influence); 'they came around eventually' (no specific moment)
Senior signal: describe the skeptic charitably — as a valuable source of critique, not as an obstacle

questions.question: Describe a time you had to align multiple teams on a platform direction when they had competing interests. How did you resolve it?
key_points: Specific competing interests — what each team actually wanted and why, not generic 'different opinions'
Your map of negotiability: whose constraints were hard, whose were preferences
Alignment method — usually a written RFC with options and trade-offs, or an escalation path if alignment fails
The resolution: which side won, which side lost, and how you managed the 'lost' side (platform team taking on work to reduce their cost)
Follow-through: did the alignment hold, or did it drift? Honest about the answer
Key insight: alignment isn't consensus. Sometimes it means 'we all agree team A wins, team B is unhappy, and we've named the unhappiness'
Anti-pattern: 'we had meetings and reached consensus' (vague, no method)

questions.question: What's an opinionated default in a platform you've built or used that turned out to be the wrong opinion?
key_points: Pick a default with organizational consequences, not just technical ones

Examples: a required language that didn't survive a technology shift; a default observability baseline that over- or under-instrumented; a scaffolder so easy it encouraged microservice sprawl; a CODEOWNERS default assigning reviews to a team that couldn't keep up
Describe the signal that told you it was wrong
What you changed, and the trade-off you accepted in the replacement — every fix is a new opinion that will also age
Lesson about how you'd spot this pattern earlier next time
Avoid: 'all our defaults were fine' (not credible); minor examples (100m vs 50m CPU)
Senior signal: acknowledge that the default was right at the time; it's the evolution that made it wrong

questions.question: Tell me about something you deprecated — a tool, service, or platform capability. How did you handle it?
key_points: The signal for deprecation — why now? Usually: maintenance debt exceeds value, newer capability replaces, or the original use case has evolved
Stakeholder map — who was using it, who'd be affected, who'd be relieved
Migration support from the platform team — not just 'we have docs,' but something concrete (writing the PRs for the thin users, pair-programming with medium users)
The edge case that held things up — there's always one; describe it honestly
Timeline: did you hit the original deadline or slip? How did you manage slippage?
Final flip — what happened on shutdown day
Hardest conversation — often with security or compliance wanting faster shutdown vs teams needing more time
Anti-pattern: 'we announced deprecation and gave 90 days' (reveals no real stakeholder work)
